speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. You were telling me about your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, well, that's perhaps important. You wanted to know names of people I got the instructions for to get them out of the camp. One was a very important one. And that's where I got the instruction from, from London. And the instruction was, keep him out of the camp, and let him go under, because he will be the one who is going to make the new civil code for the country after the war. And that was Professor [? Myers ?]. He was the head of the-- before the war-- head of the international court in The Hague. And another member of his family, and she was-- that particular woman, the wife of that friend, family. She was very important in the show business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Kerido, ?] That was the other one. So I called him into my office and told him what my instructions were. Well, he didn't know much of me. Yeah, he knew my-- as a person who was working in the camp, but not what-- how I was and, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he looked at it, and said, well, may I have some time to think it over? I said, certainly. Why not? And a couple of days after, he came to me. He said, no. I have decided not to go outside the camp. I'll stay here. So I never questioned him during that period of time. That was in August 1943. And actually, he was sent to Theresienstadt in September 1994-- 1944. So I thought, well, then I'm-- now he's outside of the country. What can I do now? But they were-- or the Germans said at that particular time that these people, because they were important for Holland, that they were treating them differently. For that reason, they sent them over to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the war, he was liberated there. And he organized the back transport of all the other Dutch people who are still living in that township back to Holland. And his daughter, she was in-- also a lawyer. He was a lawyer and her daughter, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't he want to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- well, that's the question where I come to. So in August 1945, I went with the last transport-- but because my promise was I bring the Jewish people back into their own community afterwards. That was my instruction, and I wanted to finish that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the last transport back home for these people. I went with them. And went over then to Rotterdam, where my wife's parents lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: But from there on, I visited quite a number of, of people whom I knew in the camp. So I saw first then [? Kerido, ?] and he was working in the hospital. He was a doctor. Later on, he became professor. And we had a chat there together about all things. He said, do you still want to see my uncle, Professor [? Myers? ?] I said, yes, because he was living opposite the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: So together, we went over to the other side of the road. And that's where we had a chat together. And before I left, I said to him, tell me, Professor, what was now the main reason why you didn't want to go out of, out of the camp when I asked you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, well-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm a very well-known person in the country. So many more people know me than I know them. So I didn't think it was fair for the people, where I had to go under and these people had to look after me, that others were not knowing that I was around. And then it was quite easy to get someone who wanted to make it known to the Germans, and re-arrest me, [? I thought. ?] That's why he didn't want to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Kerido?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Kerido, ?] the same. Yeah, [? Kerido ?] well, he stayed because of his uncle. But he never explained it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you get [? people ?] out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told you already one. That was the-- when I had the office outside the camp. So to lock them in during the day, I got a pass for them to, to come and visit my office. I could write these passes out. Nobody was checking them. And because I always had an excuse for it, where did you leave your coupons, and-- it was quite an easy explanation. So most of these people, I asked to come in, say, about 2 or 3 o'clock in the afternoon. The office was closing about 4:00 or 5:00. And kept them there. And I locked the office in the evening, 5 o'clock. Close it. And out of the window, they went. [INAUDIBLE] lie down. That nobody can see you, as long as it's light. That was my instruction to them. And as soon as the dark fell, you can jump out. And they were free.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would happen to them from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, that was their own business. Sometimes, they had an address. Sometimes, they went back to their own family, which wasn't picked up at that particular moment. And from there on, they-- some people went even-- I had one that was a nurse. And she was working in the hospital part time and part time in my office. And one day, she came to me. She said, Mr. Van As, I'm going under. I said. OK. Go ahead. And she said, do you-- don't make it known to the Germans until, say, 24 hours later. I said, I'm going to tell anybody about it. I don't know anything about it. You work in the camp. You work here. OK. What you are doing next to it is your business. I don't want to know it even.

speaker: interviewee
text: So instead of walking into the camp, she walked just out, passed the [? fire. ?] And had the luck to go over to England. And that's where she joined the, the Dutch army there [? referred ?] her, in the army. Was she Jewish? Was she Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. She was Jewish. Another one, which I didn't help at all, but I know the story, he just walked out the camp. It was that easy. I mean, there was not very much control. It [? wasn't ?] very hard at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no fence around the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. At that time, the fence was pulled around in-- and the watch towers were put, built, brought up on the outside-- July, July '42. That's where they started off, yeah, to put barbed wire around it. But them that's why I said the controlling element of it was not hard at all. So the only thing is, [? what ?] later on, the Germans-- the same as what they did to us outside-- if I go to leave the country, and we get to know it, OK. We take measurements against your family. And that's what they said in the camp, too. If you are going out the camp, well, you put your family at risk. So that's why not many people actually left . And in the camp itself, well, they were quite happy, so far possible, of course. Because they had their [? feet ?]. And everything was quite, so far, normal. And it wasn't hard at all for them. It was just the transports, the fright for the transport. Next week, we might be transported to Poland too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many transports were there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what it was like the night before the transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was hopeless. It was always a fight to get them off the list. They-- the first instruction was always-- the transport was on the Tuesday.

speaker: interviewee
text: So by Thursday, that's where we got to know what is going to happen next Tuesday, how many people they want there. And that's where [? for ?] [? new, ?] the fights always started. So what Schlesinger did was bring in-- he had a card. In the card register, he had, say, people who were, straight away, available for transport. [? Sections. ?] But if he couldn't make that total up, he had to look for others who were in the system of working in the camp itself and had already certain secure position there that there was no risk for go on transport. But if there was shortage, yes, well-- but who? Yeah? Who is going to be put on transport? So the first selection was [? done. ?] Then, I'll take now, how many people are available? What are they short of? And who-- whom are they going to pick there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, sometimes-- well, that's what I said already before-- Schlesinger had some people he wanted to get rid of. They were a nuisance to him. Or other leaders there, for, for groups, they wanted to get rid of some people. So these were taken first. And then you had the others. And that's what I had to watch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of anybody that he wanted to get rid of that went on transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not offhand, no. But I know that it happened. Certainly I do. But in, in this sort of system, then you had to watch [? tower-- ?] there was another person that was, Dr. [? Otterstein. ?] He was the one who had to look after persons for which there was an application to be-- not to go on transport, that they had to prove those things. And that had to be sent over to the German authorities to get an approval for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these people were already taken out too. And [? Dr. Otterstein ?] had to watch that part. I had to watch my part. And so were other people in the camp still, who had to watch out for those people-- after their own people, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, then ultimately, you had your-- you had a first, first list. And that's where the selection took part. Then, you had a final list. And eventually, if there was a shortage, an additional list.

speaker: interviewee
text: So during that period of time, I got first on the first list. Then I got the corrected list, and then I got the additional list for the last one, just came in. So you had to watch it during the whole night to see if they were going to go to put on people you didn't want to go. Oh, yeah. And then you had your transport. And that's where the whole camp was always dumbfounded. We said, yeah. We lost it again. Can you tell me about the atmosphere in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hopeless. That was hopeless always. [? SS-- ?] you were in the states that there is no chance anymore. That's where the whole lot quieted down. They were dumbfounded. They're sitting there. Yeah, well. That's now happened what is going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the procedure on a Tuesday morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a Tuesday morning, then they'll-- it was always done by their own people.

speaker: interviewer
text: By?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish [? OD, ?] the police organization. And from the hospital, the nurses, they were going to bring those people in who were then selected for transport. And as soon as that was completed-- now, what my task was, was always, not to get the numbers. So up till the last moment, I was trying to get persons out. And that happened actually with Heinus Eidefeld, a boy [? who ?] [? I ?] [? fostered ?] there for about two and a half years. And his parents were on transport, and he was already in the train. And I got him out at the last moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Get him out? Yeah, well, it's a matter of some, some acting. And where you acted, it didn't always-- later on, finally they realized, hey, he did it. How could he do it? But it was done. It happened. And then try to get them safeguarded that he doesn't go on running a chance of going on transport anymore. So that's where he got out at the last moment. And I was, at that moment, already back in my office. And he came running in. I see [? that ?] all [? that ?] happening. And he knelt down in front of me and started crying because his parents were still in the train, and he was the only one and was a boy of 16 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's why I took care of him for the rest of the time that he was in Westerbork. And still, he-- at long last, he left on his own free will. I, I still think about him quite a lot. And we quite often discussed that-- because [? SS ?] something is on television again. Then, these histories repeat itself. What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was sent over-- [COUGHS] The only thing that I could do there is got him on, on a transport. Because he wanted to go to Poland. He was Polish originally, was brought up in Holland, so he spoke his Dutch very well. And his parents didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what he was all the time actually saying to us, yeah, but as soon as my parents-- and if the liberation is there, blah, blah, blah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLEARS THROAT] Then I want to do something for my parents and get them back in Holland again because they don't speak the language. And I could take care that they come back here, and so on. But it brought it [? then ?] [? until ?] we had already the invasion into Europe by the English. And it became, actually, already see-able that we were liberated in a very short time. And that's where these last transports took place. Now, there was a transport for Auschwitz and a transport for Theresienstadt. Auschwitz left the first. That was on the third of September. And Theresienstadt left on the fourth of September. And I got him-- no, no. I got him earlier. I got him in February. That's when he left already, in February, 1944. A day before my wife's birthday. And I got him there on transport to, to Theresienstadt. But he-- but I heard afterwards, from the Red Cross, because I asked by the Red Cross later on, after liberation, what happened to several people, and one was Heinus Eidefeld. And he was in Theresienstadt for about a week, and then further transported back to-- or also to Auschwitz, and [? whatever ?] happened. He was together with a friend of his. And his brother is still living here, off the train. He lives in [INAUDIBLE]. And that one died in Bergen-Belsen. And they both together went to Theresienstadt-- to Auschwitz after Theresienstadt. And I had lost all trace of Heinus after Auschwitz. But from [? Felix, ?] we got to know that he had the-- perhaps you've heard the, the death walk from Auschwitz after the Russians controlled the-- Poland. And that was the big march from all these prisoners through Germany. And he ended up in Bergen-Belsen. That's where he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to the transports, did people know where they were going? Did they know where the trains were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- in the middle of the night, after 12 o'clock, then they went over to-- in the first instance, then to the bigger barracks. And if they were living in the smaller houses there, than they knocked them on the door that you had to prepare yourself for transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: What could they take with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I took care of was in-- always that-- I kept them as inhabitants of the camp for a further three days. So instead of dropping their state of the registration for the coupons, I kept them on for a further three days so-- because it was said to us anyhow, that it took them three days from Holland to Auschwitz and that rations were given to them in the train. So, so that was-- the food pile side, that was my business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And clothing, did they take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what they could carry. I don't know if there was a certain limit, but they had quite some luggage with them. I know one of my-- the people of my office in-- who had to go. And that was, yeah, also something. But I still can't overcome myself. He was the leader of my office. And the day before that something happened, he was put on the list anyhow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I tried to get him off, just the usual way. And I rang Schlesinger. I said, look, Schlesinger, blah, blah, blah, tried to get him off.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Schlesinger had, at that particular time, Gemmeker in his office. And instead of answering me, he handed the telephone over to Gemmeker. So instead of further answer to my question, I got, Gemmeker, what do you want, to me. And I had to tell him the story, which was, which was bad, of course. And then he said to me, Nein. And I never forgotten that sentence either. Das ist absicht. And das ist absicht means, that's my intention. I have done it already. And so what he had against him was purely, apparently, to get me. They were after me already for a long time. I knew that. But that was a different-- you are trying to trace it to-- how could it happen that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: [COUGHS] I still wasn't satisfied with that answer. And I tried to get him off the list anyhow alongside another way. And I couldn't get into the office where the lists were, list were typed. But anyhow, I did it. And it was late in the evening. And it was rainy, and it was stormy. So I went into the office, and there was [? Fritz. ?] [? Fritz ?] was the one who was the controlling officer there. And I talked it over with [? Fritz. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, suddenly, the door opened. And who came in there? Gemmeker. Now, I was on the spot where I wasn't to be expected. And what happened, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, it was rainy, [? like ?] [? I ?] [? said. ?] And he took his cap off. And he said [SPEAKING GERMAN]. What bad rainy weather, he said. And I just got around him, out of the office, and went out. He never approached me for it, that I was there. He must have seen me [? coming ?] [? out. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he knew you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, he knew me. Of course. And I went home to tell my wife what happened. After that, I went back into the office again for the further of the night. And at that time, that chap came in-- the, that particular person who wanted I to get off.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spier. And he said, Mr. Van As, I've heard it. You've tried everything, but nothing can help me anymore. I'm really gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had a pair of shoes, ski shoes, heavy ones. And he said, do you want have them? I said, no. No. You may use them, perhaps, may have a better use for it than I have because what we expected was that he'd do hard work there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, no. I'm not going to take them with me. And you, if you are not going to take them, then I cut them up. I said, well, that's up to you. But I don't want them. I never wanted anything anyhow. So that was the last thing I know of him. And as far as I know, he was three days after that. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody ever offer your payment to save them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever offer your payment to save their lives? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as far as I know, because I didn't do any-- it was not a question. They would come up to me. I had to help them, and that was it. No. I don't know-- even for an offer. Sometimes they were asking if he would care for some money of them in the camp. That, that happened quite often. Yeah. That-- because they couldn't get hold of the Dutch currency. In the camp itself, they had their own currency printed, which they could buy with their own money, real money. But the camp money wasn't, wasn't of any value outside the camp. And for some people, we took care of some money there until they needed it. But no. I know of persons who were, were only doing things for, for money. And that was one thing. There was once-- there was a secret police also in the camp who was checking-- the secret police and police in the camp-- secret police checking on Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the secret police? Also, Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Jew, yeah. And he came to me and offered me if I wanted to buy a fur coat for my wife. He said, you can have the cheapest. For at no price at all, I don't want it. [CLEARS THROAT] And then he went off to my wife, and she just told him the same. And a couple days thereafter, I saw one of the Dutch police who were, was transferred into German service. His wife was getting it. Yeah. Oh, well. [CLEARS THROAT] And I know that-- he was working in the kitchen, he was a cook-- that he took stuff out of the kitchen, which he stole from Jewish people and paid for it with the, with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people pay to be kept off the transport lists? Did they pay or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. There were several lists. We had [? liner ?] [? up ?] list. That's where people had to pay, say, 100 guilders to get on that list. We had a diamond [INAUDIBLE] that was the diamond list. That was for people who were working in the diamond industry. And they paid with diamonds to the Germans to get on that list. Yeah. So there were several lists. And yeah, I don't remember all the names. But anyhow, the Germans were trying to get as much hold of their possessions as possible. If it was in money, if it was in jewelry, or in diamonds, as long as they got hold of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they paid for work positions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only, only for getting a temporary suspension from transport. And it was only temporary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- who did they pay that to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the German authorities. That all went to the Germans. There was-- in the camp, [? SS ?] transport-- people were coming into the camp. There was a Jewish bank, Lippmann and Rosenthal, which was, of course, taken over by the Germans too. And they were collecting all the possession in monies, in jewelry, for people who were arriving with those things in the camp. Now, well, what they couldn't hide, well, they lost it to the Lippmann and Roosevelt bank.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was as soon as they came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. After they were registered, they were moved over to the-- this person from Lippmann and Rosenthal bank. And they searched them-- find out anyhow if they were wearing jewelry, rings, and other things, possessions and what they had already in their coats hidden, and whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that done by Jews or by Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was-- these were Dutch people. But I think these were from the NSB, the Nazi organization in Holland. And they were not too friendly, of course, in handling their business. But people-- most of the people knew already from others who had the experience before, think about it. Watch it. As soon as you pass the registration, you're coming in front of these people from the bank. Be careful, and don't show anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: [COUGHS] And what happened to their luggage when they came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the luggage was their luggage. It was never searched.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they could take whatever they brought with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know, sometimes-- and that was a typical thing. And I always have said that is typical Jewish, because that you find that in, in the, in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mitzvah, right? Perhaps that would be the best expression. Quite a number of people were sometimes picked up without anything at all. We got people out of another concentration camp, Amersfoort, hardly anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: The clothes they were wearing was in prisoner clothes and no socks, wooden shoes. That was all they had. We had them from [INAUDIBLE]. And these people were badly treated there. But that's why they found always Westerbork as a haven, as a heaven instead of the hell they went through. And within 24 hours, they were fully dressed in the camp from what people were giving to those people. They said, yeah. Our, our sewing machine was used quite often for that sort of business, to remake dresses which were too small, or too wide, to make them fit. That was done in our unit. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to have Jewish people come to visit you in your unit? We always had the-- that's, that was a typical thing in the camp. Not during the daytime, of course. But then, there were always Germans walking around, the Germans from the office of Gemmeker. So then they could see what you did. But this always happened in night time. Yeah. We had always visitors. And what, what else was camp life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, I was living for two years in between them. My neighbors were Jewish people on both sides. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll break there for the end of the tape.
